reasoning: The resolution criterion requires South Korea's 2025 Total Fertility Rate (TFR) to be strictly greater than its 2023 TFR of 0.72. The evidence strongly supports a YES resolution. First, preliminary data for 2024 reports that TFR already rebounded to 0.75, which leaves a comfortable margin above the 0.72 threshold. Second, data from the first five months of 2025 shows robust continued growth, with a 6.9% year-over-year increase in absolute births compared to 2024 (including a 14-year high growth rate in May). Because pregnancies are 9 months long, the births for Q3 2025 are already largely determined and expected to continue the "echo boomer" / post-COVID marriage rebound trend. To resolve NO, 2025 TFR would have to drop to 0.72 or below, which would require an unprecedented crash in births in the second half of 2025—so large that it reverses an 11-month streak of growth and drags the full-year average below 2023 numbers. Adding the fact that the denominator for TFR (women of childbearing age) is shrinking each year means that even flat reproduction in absolute numbers yields a higher TFR. I assign 95% due to the system's confidence limits, though structural factors almost guarantee the rate stays above 0.72 in 2025.
